content: Incidence measures the rate of new cases of a disease occurring in a population during a specified time period, expressed as cases per person-time (e.g., per 1,000 person-years). It captures disease onset and is essential for understanding disease risk and etiology. Prevalence measures the total number of existing cases (both new and pre-existing) in a population at a specific point in time or over a period, expressed as a proportion (e.g., 5% of the population). Prevalence reflects disease burden and is influenced by both incidence and disease duration. High prevalence with low incidence suggests chronic, long-duration conditions (e.g., diabetes), while high incidence with low prevalence suggests acute, short-duration conditions (e.g., influenza). Public health planning uses incidence for prevention strategies and prevalence for resource allocation and healthcare service planning.

In public health practice, incidence and prevalence guide different intervention strategies. Incidence data drives primary prevention efforts: vaccination campaigns target diseases with high incidence rates; screening programs prioritize conditions where early detection reduces incidence of advanced disease; outbreak investigations focus on identifying sources when incidence spikes unexpectedly. Prevalence data informs healthcare infrastructure: chronic disease management programs scale based on prevalence estimates; medication stockpiles align with prevalent conditions; specialist workforce planning considers prevalent disease burden. The relationship Prevalence ≈ Incidence × Duration helps estimate missing parameters when one measure is unavailable. For acute outbreaks, incidence rates track epidemic curves and intervention effectiveness. For chronic diseases, prevalence surveys (e.g., NHANES) monitor population health trends. Misinterpreting these measures leads to flawed conclusions: declining prevalence may reflect increased mortality rather than improved prevention; stable prevalence with rising incidence suggests improved survival.

Accurate measurement of incidence requires: (1) clear case definition with standardized diagnostic criteria; (2) defined population at risk (excluding those with prior disease); (3) complete case ascertainment through surveillance systems or active case finding; (4) accurate denominator data (person-time at risk). Common biases include: under-ascertainment (missing cases), especially for asymptomatic conditions; changes in diagnostic criteria over time artificially altering incidence trends; migration affecting denominator accuracy. Prevalence measurement requires: (1) representative sampling or complete population enumeration; (2) valid diagnostic tools applied consistently; (3) timing specification (point prevalence vs period prevalence). Biases include: survival bias (prevalent cases are survivors, not incident cases); recall bias in self-reported surveys; non-response bias if diseased individuals are less likely to participate. Period prevalence (cases existing during a time interval) exceeds point prevalence and approximates incidence for short-duration diseases. Administrative data (ICD codes) often underestimate true incidence/prevalence due to coding errors and incomplete capture of undiagnosed cases.
